topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

The small, awkwardly wrapped box sat on my desk, an insult masquerading as a gesture. I stared at it, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. Liam. That boy, who used to doodle on my lecture notes, now decided to drop off artisanal chocolates. I picked up the box with two fingers, the cheap paper crinkling under my touch like dry leaves. My hands were shaking, a low, vibrating tension pooling in my chest. I wanted to slam it down, to watch the contents scatter across the floor, but some ridiculous, hot surge of something else held me back. I just wanted him to understand that his pathetic attempts at reconciliation were utterly misplaced.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the word scraping out of my throat like gravel. The gift—a ridiculously oversized, brightly colored sketchbook—was placed squarely in the middle of my meticulously organized filing cabinet. I slammed my palm against the cool metal, the sound echoing sharply in the otherwise silent classroom. My breath hitched, short and sharp. I could feel the heat rising up my neck, a furious pressure behind my eyes. It was the audacity of it all that truly set me off; the sheer, unwarranted presumption of his return. I didn't even bother looking at the wrapping paper; I just needed to get rid of it, to erase the evidence of his presence.

[story 3]

I picked up the package as if it were contaminated. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming. It was Marcus, the one who always seemed to look down his nose at my authority. Now he presented me with this saccharine offering. My knuckles were white where I gripped the cardboard. I took a slow, deliberate step back, putting distance between myself and the offending item. My voice, when I finally spoke, was dangerously low, a strained whisper that held more threat than a shout. "Take that away," I managed, my gaze locked on the box, burning with a cold, hard intensity.

I picked up the package as if it were contaminated. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming. My voice, when I finally spoke, was dangerously low, a strained whisper that held more threat than a shout. "Take that away," I managed, my gaze locked on the box, burning with a cold, hard intensity.
